policy_statement: Hiroshima City targets household fuel cell installations of 15,000 by FY2020 and 56,000 by FY2030.
mechanism_description: Installing household fuel cells enables more efficient on-site household energy generation, which lowers building-related energy emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: targets household fuel cell installations of 15,000 by FY2020 and 56,000 by FY2030
primary_causal_pathway: Household energy supply technology change → ↑ on-site conversion efficiency / ↓ grid and boiler energy demand → ↓ building GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes the building energy system by promoting installation of household fuel cells that co-generate electricity and usable heat at the home. (2) The mediating physical process is more efficient fuel-to-energy conversion, often through combined heat and power operation that captures waste heat and displaces separately produced grid electricity and combustion-based water or space heating. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower operational greenhouse gas emissions from buildings, assuming the fuel cell system has lower lifecycle or operational emissions than the displaced energy supply mix.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies deploying household fuel cells is reduced building-sector greenhouse gas emissions by supplying household heat and electricity more efficiently on-site.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: household fuel cells alter how buildings obtain electricity and heat, increasing efficiency and thereby lowering operational emissions from household energy use.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: although the mechanism can be framed as improved energy efficiency, the classification rule asks for the single biggest climate-relevant effect. That effect is emissions reduction, so Mitigation is primary rather than Resource Efficiency. It is not Adaptation because any resilience benefit from distributed generation is secondary and context-specific. It is not Nature-Based Solutions because no ecosystem enhancement is involved.  
3. Edge cases applied: classification could vary if fuel cells are supplied with high-carbon fossil hydrogen or natural gas in a very low-carbon grid context, in which case emissions benefits may shrink or disappear. However, the canonical mechanism as stated is specifically “building energy emissions reduction,” so the intended shared mechanism is mitigation through lower emissions.  
4. Confirm applicability to all instances: for any policy genuinely sharing this mechanism, the common feature is deployment of household fuel cells for lower-emission building energy supply. That means the primary category remains Mitigation across instances, even if co-benefits such as efficiency, resilience, or innovation differ by jurisdiction.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment deferred: finer distinctions such as whether the policy is energy efficiency, distributed energy, fuel switching, CHP, hydrogen, or resilience-supporting should be assigned later using policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Household fuel cells sit near the boundary between Mitigation and Resource Efficiency because they often work by improving end-use energy efficiency through cogeneration. Some deployments may also claim resilience benefits from distributed generation. In addition, actual emissions outcomes depend strongly on the fuel source, grid carbon intensity, and system performance. Nevertheless, because the canonical mechanism explicitly specifies building energy emissions reduction, Mitigation is the correct primary classification.
key_indicators: "targets"; "household fuel cell installations"; "15,000 by FY2020"; "56,000 by FY2030"; "Number of introduced household fuel cells (Total)"
co_benefits: Potential energy resilience or backup power benefits during outages associated with typhoons, flooding, or heat events, but these are not explicitly stated and do not meet the threshold for a secondary category.
instance_edge_case_notes: The text is a bare target table entry and does not specify whether the target is legally binding or linked to subsidies, financing, or broader implementation programs. Household fuel cells may have variable emissions benefits depending on fuel source and displaced grid mix, but the assigned primary mitigation classification is retained.
